---
title: "Research"
---

## VSMC Phenotypic Plasticity

Vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs) are highly plastic cells capable of transitioning between a quiescent **contractile phenotype** and a proliferative **synthetic phenotype** — a switch central to the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis and in-stent restenosis.

My primary doctoral project investigates the transcriptional basis of this switch through an integrated computational–experimental framework:

- **Discovery** — Donor-aware modelling of public human aortic SMC transcriptomic data to delineate a phenotypic switching program.
- **Transferability** — Evaluation of the program across independent perturbation contexts (growth-factor, cytokine, and metabolic/stress stimuli).
- **Human plaque anchoring** — Cross-referencing with published human atherosclerotic plaque single-cell datasets.
- **Experimental validation** — Perturbation models in primary human aortic SMCs (HAoSMC), paired with pharmacological counter-stimuli to test the functional identity of the program.

**Key tools**: R (limma, WGCNA, clusterProfiler, GSVA), Western Blot, Immunofluorescence, RT-qPCR.

---

## ABCB5⁺ MSCs & Chronic Wound Healing

ABCB5⁺ MSCs are a rare population of skin-derived stromal cells defined by expression of the ATP-binding cassette transporter ABCB5, with immunomodulatory and tissue-regenerative properties.

This line of research has two components:

**Published review** — A comprehensive review of the differentiation and regeneration potential of ABCB5⁺ MSCs and their clinical translation perspectives, published in *Journal of Clinical Medicine* (2025).

**Original bioinformatics study** — A systems-level multi-modal analysis of the paracrine role of ABCB5⁺ MSCs in chronic wound repair, integrating:

- Expression-based paracrine candidate identification
- Chronic wound transcriptome profiling (co-expression network analysis)
- Single-cell RNA-seq contextualization and cell–cell communication analysis
- Immune micro-environment profiling
- Causal-inference and structural plausibility analyses

Computational findings are complemented by experimental co-culture studies examining the paracrine effects of ABCB5⁺ MSCs on wound-relevant cell populations. Manuscript in preparation.

---

## Endocytic Trafficking in HeLa Cells

Working within the [Starkuvienė Lab](https://www.bioquant.uni-heidelberg.de/groups/starkuviene) at BioQuant, I investigate endocytic pathway regulation in HeLa Kyoto cells using:

- **Size-selective dextran-FITC uptake assays** — fluid-phase endocytosis quantification across molecular-weight ranges
- **Pathway-specific pharmacological inhibition** — dissecting macropinocytic and dynamin-dependent endocytic contributions
- **3D spheroid culture models** — ultra-low-attachment platforms for 3D-context validation
- **Wound healing migration assays** — scratch assay with quantitative imaging analysis

This work contributes to the lab's broader programme on the spatial and temporal regulation of intracellular trafficking and its dysregulation in disease and aging.

---

## Methods Overview

| Category | Techniques |
|----------|-----------|
| **Bioinformatics** | Bulk RNA-seq (limma, edgeR), scRNA-seq (Seurat), WGCNA, GSVA, GSEA, Mendelian Randomization (TwoSampleMR, coloc), CellChat, ssGSEA, molecular docking (HDOCK) |
| **Wet Lab** | Western Blot, Immunofluorescence, RT-qPCR, Wound Healing Assay, Cell Viability (CTB), Dextran Uptake Assay, 3D Spheroid Culture |
| **Imaging** | Widefield fluorescence microscopy, Confocal microscopy (Z-stack), Quantitative image analysis (ImageJ) |
| **Cell Models** | Primary human aortic SMCs (HAoSMC), HeLa cells, ABCB5⁺ MSCs |
| **Languages** | R, Python |
